---
title: "Reproducible Data Analysis"
author: "Inayat Roy"
date: today
format:
  pdf:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Busiest Airports Analysis

```{r}
#| label: airports-data

library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
library(patchwork)

# Passenger data sourced from Wikipedia:
# List of Busiest Airports by Passenger Traffic (ACI figures)
airports <- data.frame(
  Code = c("ATL", "FRA", "PKX", "DXB", "LAX", "LHR"),
  Name = c("Atlanta", "Frankfurt", "Beijing Daxing",
           "Dubai", "Los Angeles", "London Heathrow"),
  `2020` = c(42918685, 18768601, NA, 25836771, 28779527, 22111469),
  `2021` = c(75704760, 24812849, 25051012, 29110609, 48007284, NA),
  `2022` = c(93699630, 48918482, NA, 66069981, 65924298, 61614508),
  `2023` = c(104653451, 59355389, 39400000, 86994365, 75050875, 79183364),
  `2024` = c(108067766, 61564957, 49441029, 92331506, 76588028, 83884572),
  check.names = FALSE
)

# Reshape to long format for plotting
airports_long <- airports %>%
  select(-Code) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -Name,
    names_to = "Year",
    values_to = "Passengers"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Year = as.integer(Year),
    Passengers_M = Passengers / 1000000
  )
```

## Overview

This section examines passenger traffic trends across six major international
airports from 2020 to 2024. The years covered span the COVID-19 pandemic's
impact on air travel through the subsequent recovery period. The six airports
selected — Atlanta (ATL), Frankfurt (FRA), Beijing Daxing (PKX), Dubai (DXB),
Los Angeles (LAX), and London Heathrow (LHR) — represent key hubs across
North America, Europe, the Middle East, and Asia.

## Passenger Traffic Table

```{r}
#| label: airports-table

airports %>%
  select(-Code) %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ ifelse(is.na(.), "N/A",
    formatC(., format = "f", digits = 0, big.mark = ",")))) %>%
  kable(
    caption = "Annual Passenger Traffic for Six Major Airports (2020-2024)",
    col.names = c("Airport", "2020", "2021", "2022", "2023", "2024")
  )
```

The table reveals the dramatic collapse of passenger traffic in 2020 due to
the COVID-19 pandemic, followed by a strong and consistent recovery through
2024. Atlanta consistently led all airports in total passengers, reaching over
108 million in 2024. Beijing Daxing (PKX) did not appear in the top 50 in 2020
and 2022, reflecting China's strict border restrictions during those years.
London Heathrow similarly fell outside the top 50 in 2021 due to the UK's
travel restrictions.

## Passenger Traffic Over Time

```{r}
#| label: airports-plot
#| fig-cap: "Passenger traffic (in millions) for six major airports, 2020-2024."
#| fig-height: 4

ggplot(airports_long, aes(x = Year, y = Passengers_M,
                          color = Name, group = Name)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  labs(
    title = "Passenger Traffic Recovery at Six Major Airports (2020-2024)",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Passengers (Millions)",
    color = "Airport"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2020:2024)
```

The line graph makes the recovery trajectory clear for each airport. All six
airports experienced sharp declines in 2020, with Dubai and Heathrow among the
hardest hit. By 2023 and 2024, most airports had not only recovered but
surpassed pre-pandemic levels. Atlanta's consistent dominance is visually
apparent, while Beijing Daxing shows the steepest growth curve as it ramped
up operations from a near-standstill.

# Monte Carlo Numerical Integration

```{r}
#| label: mc-setup

set.seed(42)

# Monte Carlo simulation function for Beta(2,2) PDF
# x bounds: 0 to 1, y bounds: 0 to 1.5
run_mc <- function(n, shape1 = 2, shape2 = 2,
                   x_min = 0, x_max = 1,
                   y_min = 0, y_max = 1.5) {
  x_vals <- runif(n, x_min, x_max)
  y_vals <- runif(n, y_min, y_max)
  below <- y_vals <= dbeta(x_vals, shape1, shape2)
  area <- (x_max - x_min) * (y_max - y_min)
  estimate <- mean(below) * area
  data.frame(x = x_vals, y = y_vals, below = below,
             estimate = estimate, n = n)
}

# Run at four resolutions
mc10    <- run_mc(10)
mc100   <- run_mc(100)
mc1000  <- run_mc(1000)
mc10000 <- run_mc(10000)

# Function to make a single panel plot
make_mc_plot <- function(df) {
  est <- round(unique(df$estimate), 4)
  n   <- unique(df$n)
  x_seq <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 300)
  curve_df <- data.frame(x = x_seq,
                         y = dbeta(x_seq, shape1 = 2, shape2 = 2))

  ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y, color = below)) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 0.8) +
    geom_line(data = curve_df, aes(x = x, y = y),
              color = "black", linewidth = 1, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
    scale_color_manual(
      values = c("TRUE" = "steelblue", "FALSE" = "tomato")) +
    labs(
      title = paste0("n = ", formatC(n, format = "d", big.mark = ",")),
      subtitle = paste0("Estimate: ", est),
      x = "x", y = "y"
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(legend.position = "none")
}
```

## Method Overview

Monte Carlo numerical integration estimates the value of a definite integral
using random sampling. The approach works by generating random points within
a rectangular window that bounds the function, then calculating the proportion
of points that fall at or below the curve. Multiplying this proportion by the
area of the rectangle yields an estimate of the integral. As the number of
sampled points increases, the estimate converges toward the true value.

For this analysis, the function being integrated is the probability density
function of the Beta distribution with shape parameters $\alpha = 2$ and
$\beta = 2$, integrated over $x \in [0, 1]$ with a rectangular window of
$y \in [0, 1.5]$.

## Small Multiple Visualization

```{r}
#| label: mc-plots
#| fig-cap: "Monte Carlo integration of the Beta(2,2) PDF at four resolutions."
#| fig-height: 6

plot10    <- make_mc_plot(mc10)
plot100   <- make_mc_plot(mc100)
plot1000  <- make_mc_plot(mc1000)
plot10000 <- make_mc_plot(mc10000)

(plot10 + plot100) / (plot1000 + plot10000)
```

## Results and Discussion

The small multiple visualization demonstrates how the Monte Carlo estimate
improves with more simulated points. At $n = 10$, the estimate is highly
variable and unreliable. By $n = 1{,}000$ and $n = 10{,}000$, the estimate
stabilizes and approaches the true value of 1.0, which is expected since the
Beta distribution PDF integrates to exactly 1 over $[0, 1]$. This illustrates
a core principle of Monte Carlo methods: precision improves with resolution.

# Planning and Prompting GenAI Tools

## Data Context and Plan

**Goals:** The calcium dataset is currently in a wide format, which makes it
harder to analyze trends properly. I want to restructure it so that each
observation is in its own row, making the data tidy and easier to work with.
My main focus is to track how calcium levels change over time and see if diet
and exercise play a role.

**Needs:**

- calcium.csv dataset
- null treatment group, diet/exercise treatment group
- Variables: Initial, Year 1, Year 2, Year 3
- Packages: tidyverse, ggplot2
- Functions: read_csv(), pivot_longer(), mutate(), bind_rows(), ggplot(),
geom_line()

**Steps:**

1. Load calcium.csv using read_csv()
2. Rename columns to identify which group they belong to
3. Separate the data into two group-specific data frames
4. Use pivot_longer() to reshape each group from wide to long format
5. Add a group label column to each reshaped data frame
6. Combine both groups into one tidy data frame using bind_rows()
7. Calculate mean calcium levels per group per year
8. Create a line plot using ggplot2 showing trends over time for each group

## Plan-Based Prompt Results

I provided Claude with my plan and the calcium.csv file. Here is the
response generated:

```r
library(tidyverse)

# Load the calcium data
calcium <- read_csv("calcium.csv")

# Rename columns to identify groups
colnames(calcium) <- c("null_y0", "null_y1", "null_y2", "null_y3",
                       "diet_y0", "diet_y1", "diet_y2", "diet_y3")

# Separate and reshape null treatment group
null_group <- calcium %>%
  select(starts_with("null")) %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  mutate(id = row_number(), group = "Null Treatment") %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = starts_with("null"),
               names_to = "year", values_to = "calcium") %>%
  mutate(year = recode(year, "null_y0" = 0, "null_y1" = 1,
                       "null_y2" = 2, "null_y3" = 3))

# Separate and reshape diet/exercise group
diet_group <- calcium %>%
  select(starts_with("diet")) %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  mutate(id = row_number(), group = "Diet/Exercise") %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = starts_with("diet"),
               names_to = "year", values_to = "calcium") %>%
  mutate(year = recode(year, "diet_y0" = 0, "diet_y1" = 1,
                       "diet_y2" = 2, "diet_y3" = 3))

# Combine groups
calcium_tidy <- bind_rows(null_group, diet_group)

# Summarise mean calcium by group and year
calcium_summary <- calcium_tidy %>%
  group_by(group, year) %>%
  summarise(mean_calcium = mean(calcium, na.rm = TRUE),
            .groups = "drop")

# Plot
ggplot(calcium_summary,
       aes(x = year, y = mean_calcium,
           color = group, group = group)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "Mean Ulnar Calcium Levels Over Time by Treatment Group",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Mean Calcium Level",
    color = "Treatment Group"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 0:3,
    labels = c("Initial", "Year 1", "Year 2", "Year 3")) +
  theme_minimal()
```

## Generic Prompt Results

The following plots were generated by Anthropic Claude using a generic
prompt without a detailed plan. The prompt simply asked Claude to analyze
the calcium dataset and create a visualization without any context or
structured guidance.

![Group means from generic Claude prompt](ClaudePlot2.png){width=80%}

![Individual lines from generic Claude prompt](ClaudePlot1.png){width=80%}

The generic response labeled the groups as Group A and Group B rather than
using the meaningful names Null Treatment and Diet/Exercise. Time points
were labeled as Wave 1 and Wave 2 rather than Year 1 and Year 2, indicating
the tool did not understand the study context without guidance.

## Comparison

When comparing the plan-informed response and the generic response, several
important differences emerge. The plan-informed response produced code that
correctly identified the two groups as Null Treatment and Diet/Exercise,
used meaningful variable names, and structured the visualization around the
specific research question of tracking calcium levels over time. The generic
response, by contrast, labeled the groups simply as Group A and Group B,
showing that without context, the tool could not interpret the meaning behind
the data structure.

The visualizations themselves also differed significantly. The plan-informed
response produced a clean line plot of group means that directly addressed
the goal of comparing trends between treatment groups. The generic response
produced a more complex plot showing both individual lines and group means,
which while visually detailed, was harder to interpret and did not clearly
communicate the research question. The generic plot also used labels like
Wave 1 and Wave 2 instead of Year 1 and Year 2, suggesting it did not
fully understand the study context without guidance.

This exercise reveals an important lesson about working with GenAI tools:
the quality of the output is directly tied to the quality of the input. A
detailed, context-specific plan guided Claude to produce code and
visualizations aligned with the actual research goals. A vague prompt
produced technically functional but contextually weak results. This shows
that GenAI tools are most powerful when used as collaborators guided by
human understanding, not as replacements for it.

# Reflection

One of the most valuable things I have learned in this course is the concept
of tidy data. Before this course, I would not have thought twice about a
dataset having multiple columns representing the same variable across
different time points. Working with the calcium dataset made this concrete —
the data had eight columns when it should have had one row per observation.
Learning to use pivot_longer() to reshape data has changed how I approach
any dataset I encounter, and I now instinctively ask whether each row
represents one observation.

I have also grown significantly in my ability to create and interpret data
visualizations using ggplot2. Early in the course, I struggled to understand
why certain plot types were more appropriate than others. Through the busiest
airports analysis, I learned that line graphs are ideal for showing change
over time, and that thoughtful labels, captions, and color choices make a
visualization genuinely useful rather than just decorative. I also learned
the importance of writing narrative text that goes beyond describing what
is visible — good analysis explains what the patterns mean and why they
matter.

Finally, the Monte Carlo integration exercise taught me that randomness can
be a powerful computational tool. I found it surprising that simply generating
random points and checking whether they fall below a curve can estimate a
definite integral. Watching the estimate converge toward 1.0 as the number
of points increased from 10 to 10,000 made the concept of statistical
precision feel real and tangible. This course has shown me that data analysis
is not just about running code — it is about thinking carefully, planning
deliberately, and communicating clearly.

# Appendices {.unnumbered}

## GenAI Usage {.unnumbered}

**Tool:** Anthropic Claude (claude.ai, Claude Sonnet 4.6)

**Date:** April 14, 2026

**Prompt 1:** I provided Claude with the calcium.csv file and the following
plan: The calcium dataset is currently in a wide format. I want to restructure
it so each observation is in its own row. My goal is to track how calcium
levels change over time and whether diet and exercise play a role. I will use
pivot_longer() to reshape the data, create a grouping variable, and use
ggplot2 to build a line plot showing trends over time for each treatment group.
I asked Claude to generate R code based on this plan.

**Response 1:** Claude generated R code that loaded the calcium.csv file,
renamed the eight columns to identify null and diet/exercise groups, separated
the data into two group-specific data frames, used pivot_longer() to reshape
each from wide to long format, combined them with bind_rows(), calculated
group means per year, and created a line plot using ggplot2 showing mean
ulnar calcium levels over time for each treatment group.

## Code Appendix {.unnumbered}

```{r}
#| label: code-appendix
#| ref.label: knitr::all_labels()
#| echo: true
#| eval: false



```
